contents.title: Original
Vie Rose
contents.description: The Vie Rose is essentially a sour-style cocktail with gin base. What makes it standout it the Kirsch and Grenadine, both offering a more complex fruit-forward flavor that enhances the drink. You will find it necessary to play with the amount of lemon juice and/or Grenadine to balance the sweetness of the cocktail.

children.value: The Vie Rose is essentially a sour-style cocktail with gin base. What makes it standout it the Kirsch and Grenadine, both offering a more complex fruit-forward flavor that enhances the drink. You will find it necessary to play with the amount of lemon juice and/or Grenadine to balance the sweetness of the cocktail.

contents.title: Savoy Smash A Not So Subtle Revisit of the Wonderful Savoy Stomp by Erik Ellestad
contents.description: Inspired by the wonderful staff at The Wells here in DC my wife bought me the recent republishing of The Savoy Cocktail Book by Henry Craddock. Like any good amateur mixologist I decided to work my way through as many of the cocktails in this book as I could. Follow along as I record successes, failures, my versions of these classic cocktails, hunts for rare ingredients, and attempts to recreate ingredients that no longer exist. Without further ado, welcome, to the Savoy Smash

children.value: A Not So Subtle Revisit of the Wonderful

by Henry Craddock. Like any good amateur mixologist I decided to work my way through as many of the cocktails in this book as I could. Follow along as I record successes, failures, my versions of these classic cocktails, hunts for rare ingredients, and attempts to recreate ingredients that no longer exist. Without further ado, welcome, to the
I am assuming that this is in reference to Green Chartreuse
For a vegan alternative you can look at aquafaba. Aquafaba is the liquid that comes in a can of chickpeas and behaves strikingly similar to egg whites.
A neutral alcohol distilled with botanicals of any sort, though the mixture must contain Juniper berries to be considered gin.

Gin comes in many styles, and is distilled all over the world. Unless specifically noted, when a recipe calls for Gin you are free to use whatever gin you fancy. However, there are a few styles that I would recommend sticking to the original if you can:
London/London Dry: This style is probably the least fussy, but does have a notably distinct flavor profile

Plymouth: Only produced in one location, though they recently (recently as of 2024) declined trade protections for the name. If the worst should happen and copycats start flooding the market, try to look for bottles distilled in Plymouth, England
Old Tom/Tom: Again, while similar to London Gin, this style tends toward sweeter so can influence the flavor of your cocktail
While there are some that are quite excellent, watch American Gin. While not legally defined, the style tends toward a flavor even more aggressive than London Dry, often overpowering cocktails.

Source of the Jamaican jerk, this liqueur was marketed as a tonic. Due to prohibition the producer was required to retain enough solids to make it unpalatable. One producer, while attempting to bypass this rule, added a compound later discovered to be a neurotoxin. This lead to thousands of consumers losing the ability to use their legs and arms.
Jamaica Ginger on The Art of Drink
A Guide to Defunct Cocktail Ingredients on Serious Eats
A similar product can be made quite easily by adding 150-300 drops of ginger oil to a bottle of high-proof alcohol, e.g. Everclear.

While still produced, though under a slightly different name of Lillet Blanc, there is some debate over the accuracy of the modern recipe and the original one. Some believe that the recipe was altered in the 70s to make it sweeter and less bitter. Erik Ellestad of the Savoy Stomp prefers Cocchi Americano when a recipe calls for Kina Lillet. Another alternative he mentions is Tempus Fugit's Kina l’Avion d’Or, which was apparently formulated specifically to replicate the older recipe of Kina Lillet.

contents.name: dash
contents.notes: 1 dash from a dasher bottle, or spritz from a spray bottle. This ends up roughly 1/16th of an oz
contents.title: Dash

contents.name: glass or wineglass
contents.notes: A historical measurement equivalent to 2 fluid ounces
contents.title: Glass

contents.name: half gill
contents.notes: Craddock chooses not to clarify what unit his cocktails are measured in (except, of course, when he does). Contemporaneous bartenders wrote their recipes using the half-gill as the default unit. A gill is 4.8 fl oz
contents.title: None
